alsoKnownAs: NOT Siamese Algae Eater (Crossocheilus oblongus) — single clean stripe reaching tail tip, clear fins, no yellow
NOT False Siamese Algae Eater / Cambodian Log Sucker (Garra cambodgiensis) — lacks yellow dorsal band

nativeRange.habitat: Fast-flowing streams and rivers with sandy or rocky substrates; also floodplain areas during seasonal floods. Found on undersides of submerged logs and floating structures. Prefers well-oxygenated, clear water with moderate to strong current.

decorPreferences: smooth rocks and flat stones for resting and territory establishment
driftwood and submerged branches (simulates natural log undersides)
dense planting along tank edges (Java fern, Anubias, Cryptocoryne)
open sandy areas mid-tank for foraging
moderate to strong current from powerhead or spray bar

diet.notes: Aufwuchs grazer in the wild, consuming algae films, small crustaceans, insect larvae, and plant detritus. In the aquarium accepts algae wafers, blanched vegetables (courgette, spinach, cucumber), and small live or frozen foods: bloodworm (chironomid larvae), daphnia, artemia. High-quality sinking pellets and spirulina-based foods are beneficial. Will graze algae from glass, rocks, and broad-leaved plants but should not be relied upon as a primary algae control method — appetite for algae diminishes with age and adequate prepared feeding.

goodWith: medium-sized barbs (e.g. tiger barbs, Denison barbs)
medium loaches (clown loach, yo-yo loach) — reported alliances with some loach species
peaceful South/Southeast Asian cichlids of similar size
bristlenose or rubber-lip plecos (different feeding style)
avoidWith: other Flying Foxes (Epalzeorhynchos kalopterus) — relentless conspecific aggression; keep only one
Siamese Algae Eaters (Crossocheilus oblongus) — targeted due to similar appearance
other Epalzeorhynchos species (E. bicolor red-tailed black shark, E. frenatum rainbow shark)
Garra species (similar body plan, provokes aggression)
slow-moving long-finned fish vulnerable to fin harassment
small peaceful fish under 4 cm that may be intimidated

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Christian Menke (Cmenke), taken 2004-09-05. 1024×974 px, 202 KB. Shows adult specimen — visible lateral stripe, fin coloration. Attribution required.

summary: A robust Southeast Asian cyprinid prized as an algae grazer but increasingly territorial and aggressive with age. Kept singly due to intense conspecific and look-alike aggression. Frequently misidentified as the Siamese Algae Eater — look for the yellow dorsal band and colored fin tips to confirm identity.
careNotes:
IDENTIFICATION — Three species are routinely confused in the trade. The Flying Fox (Epalzeorhynchos kalopterus) has two lateral stripes (one dark, one gold/yellow above it), a yellow-gold band between the dorsal fin base and caudal peduncle, and fins with black-and-red or black-tipped coloration. The true Siamese Algae Eater (Crossocheilus oblongus) has a single bold black stripe that runs cleanly from nose to the tip of the caudal fin lobes, a plain gray-brown back, and completely clear transparent fins with no yellow or red. The False Siamese Algae Eater / Cambodian Log Sucker (Garra cambodgiensis) lacks the yellow dorsal band and has a distinctly different body profile. When in doubt: if the fins are colored and you can see yellow near the dorsal base, it is a Flying Fox.

BEHAVIOR — Flying Foxes become progressively more territorial and aggressive as they mature. Juveniles sold at 3–5 cm are relatively inoffensive; adults of 12–16 cm are intensely aggressive toward any similarly patterned or similarly shaped tankmate. The aggression is not random: they specifically target fish that resemble them (other Epalzeorhynchos, Crossocheilus, Garra). Keep only one Flying Fox per tank. Some keepers report partial tolerance of unrelated loach species, but even this is individual. A 200 L minimum with a footprint of at least 120 cm provides territory for one adult and removes dead-ends from the aquascape.

ALGAE GRAZING — Flying Foxes do consume algae and will work over soft green algae, diatoms, and hair algae on rocks and glass, but they are not the workhorse algae eaters they are sometimes marketed as. True Siamese Algae Eaters are more efficient on thread/string algae (Cladophora) and red algae. Do not acquire a Flying Fox primarily for algae control.

TANK SETUP — Provide strong water movement, high oxygenation, smooth rocks, driftwood, and a tight lid. Flying Foxes are strong jumpers. Water should be soft to moderately hard (gH 2–12) and slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.0–7.5), matching their native Sundaic stream biotope. Avoid stagnant, warm, or alkaline conditions.
breedingNotes: Not bred in private aquaria under normal conditions. Commercial aquaculture uses hormone injection (HCG) to induce spawning. Sexing is difficult; ripe females may appear slightly fuller in the abdomen. No successful hobbyist breeding accounts have been reliably documented. Breeding difficulty is rated 'not in captivity' for practical aquarium purposes.

Provided adult size (140–160 mm), temperature (20–26°C), pH (6.0–7.5), hardness (36–215 ppm ≈ gH 2–12), lifespan (over 15 years potential), minimum tank footprint (120×45 cm), diet (aufwuchs, live/frozen invertebrates, blanched vegetables), temperament (increasingly territorial with age, solitary), native range (S. Thailand, Peninsular Malaysia, Borneo, Sumatra, Java), habitat (sandy/rocky stream substrate, floodplain migrations), breeding (hormone-induced commercial; not bred privately), and identification notes (white-edged red-and-black fins, two pairs of barbels).

additional.name: FishBase — Epalzeorhynchos kalopterus
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (16.0 cm TL), temperature (24–26°C), pH (6.5–7.0), hardness (5–8 dH), native range (lower Malay/Thailand peninsula, Borneo, Java, Sumatra), taxonomy (Cyprinidae, Cypriniformes, subfamily Labeoninae), diet (worms, crustaceans, insects, plant matter, trophic level 3.0 omnivore), behavior (solitary, territorial adults, minimum 100 cm aquarium). Conservation: Least Concern (IUCN 2019).

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Siamese Algae Eater Care Guide (identification section)
additional.notes: Used for SAE vs. Flying Fox ID contrast. Confirms: Flying Fox has shiny gold line flanked by two dark stripes and colored (non-clear) fins; SAE has single bold black lateral stripe and completely clear fins/tail. No dedicated Flying Fox care page found on Aquarium Co-Op at time of research.

fish.breedingNotes: Commercial breeding relies on hormone injection. No reliable private aquarium breeding documented. Hobbyists should consider this species unavailable for captive breeding purposes.
